# Edward Lorenz’s Contributions to Fluid Dynamics

## Edward Lorenz - Fluid Dynamicist Edward Lorenz was a renowned American mathematician and meteorologist whose groundbreaking work revolutionized the field of chaos theory and nonlinear dynamics. Born on May 23, 1917, in West Hartford, Connecticut, Lorenz developed a deep passion for mathematics at an early age. His insatiable curiosity and exceptional talent in the discipline laid the foundation for his extraordinary contributions to the scientific community. Throughout his illustrious career, Lorenz made significant discoveries and breakthroughs, leaving an indelible mark on the worlds of meteorology, mathematics, and physics.Early Life and Studies Edward Lorenz's journey into the world of academia began at Dartmouth College, where he received his undergraduate degree in mathematics in 1938. Dartmouth College, nestled in the picturesque town of Hanover, New Hampshire, provided Lorenz with a solid foundation in mathematical principles and critical thinking skills. Surrounded by the vibrant fall foliage and the intellectual atmosphere of the Ivy League institution, Lorenz's passion for unraveling the mysteries of the universe began to take root. His thirst for knowledge and desire to delve deeper into the intricacies of the natural world led him to pursue a Ph.D. in mathematics from Harvard University. Harvard, with its rich history and distinguished faculty, offered Lorenz an unparalleled academic environment. During his time at Harvard, Lorenz had the privilege of working under the guidance of mathematical prodigy and future Nobel laureate, George D. Birkhoff. Birkhoff's brilliance and mentorship had a profound impact on Lorenz, shaping his approach to mathematical research and instilling in him a deep appreciation for the beauty and elegance of the subject. It was at Harvard that Lorenz first started exploring the intricate relationships between mathematics and atmospheric phenomena, laying the groundwork for his lifelong fascination with weather patterns and chaotic systems. The sprawling Harvard campus, with its iconic red-brick buildings and lush green lawns, provided Lorenz with the perfect backdrop for his intellectual pursuits. In the hallowed halls of Harvard's mathematics department, he delved into the complexities of fluid dynamics and began to unravel the [mathematical underpinnings of weather prediction](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/navier-stokes-equations). After completing his doctoral studies in 1948, Lorenz embarked on a teaching career, joining the renowned faculty at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). MIT, located in the vibrant city of Cambridge, Massachusetts, was a hub of scientific innovation and intellectual curiosity. As a member of the MIT community, Lorenz found himself surrounded by brilliant minds and cutting-edge research, providing him with the ideal environment to further his scientific explorations. During his tenure at MIT, Lorenz engaged in extensive research, studying atmospheric dynamics and investigating weather prediction models. He spent countless hours in the MIT Weather Radar Laboratory, meticulously analyzing data and developing mathematical models to understand the complex behavior of the atmosphere. Lorenz's relentless dedication to his work and his unwavering curiosity led him to make groundbreaking discoveries that would forever change the field of meteorology. It was here, at MIT, that Lorenz's pioneering ideas took shape—a journey that would ultimately redefine the scientific community's understanding of the natural world. His groundbreaking research on chaos theory and the butterfly effect would revolutionize the way scientists approached the study of complex systems, transcending the boundaries of meteorology and influencing diverse fields such as physics, biology, and economics. Biggest Discoveries and Accomplishments One of Edward Lorenz's most significant contributions to science came in the early 1960s when he set out to develop a weather model to predict future weather patterns. Armed with his thorough understanding of mathematical models, Lorenz sought to simplify the complexities of atmospheric behavior. Surprisingly, he discovered that even the most minute changes in the initial conditions of a dynamic system could cause drastic differences in the long-term outcomes. This groundbreaking observation, which later became known as the "butterfly effect," revolutionized the way scientists approached the study of weather prediction. Lorenz demonstrated that tiny variations in the input parameters of mathematical models could lead to vast uncertainties in long-range forecasting. His work challenged the prevailing notion that precise and deterministic predictions were achievable, highlighting the inherent chaos and unpredictability present in nature's most complex systems. As Lorenz delved deeper into the intricacies of weather patterns, he realized that the butterfly effect was not limited to just atmospheric behavior. It extended to a wide range of natural phenomena, from the growth of forests to the formation of galaxies. This realization opened up new avenues of research for scientists across various disciplines. Furthermore, Lorenz's work had profound implications for the field of chaos theory. Prior to his discoveries, chaos was often associated with disorder and randomness. However, Lorenz's findings demonstrated that chaos could arise from deterministic systems, where small changes in initial conditions could lead to unpredictable outcomes. This concept challenged the traditional notion of cause and effect, paving the way for a deeper understanding of complex systems. Building upon his groundbreaking research, Lorenz developed a set of equations known as the Lorenz attractor. These equations provided a visual representation of chaotic behavior, resembling the wings of a butterfly. The Lorenz attractor became an iconic symbol in the field of chaos theory, capturing the essence of nonlinear dynamics and the sensitivity to initial conditions. Over the years, the butterfly effect has found applications in various fields beyond weather prediction. It has been used to study the dynamics of ecosystems, the spread of diseases, and even the behavior of financial markets. The recognition of the butterfly effect as a fundamental principle has led to the development of sophisticated mathematical models and computational techniques to better understand and predict the behavior of complex systems. Lorenz's contributions to science extended beyond his groundbreaking discoveries. He was a passionate educator and mentor, inspiring generations of scientists to explore the mysteries of chaos and complexity. His work continues to shape the way we perceive and study the world around us, reminding us of the inherent beauty and unpredictability of nature's intricate tapestry. Contributions and Legacy in Fluid Dynamics Beyond his contributions to weather prediction, Lorenz's pioneering research extended into the field of fluid dynamics. He explored the [behavior of fluids in motion](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/conservation-of-momentum-in-fluid-dynamics), uncovering intricate patterns that transcended traditional linear models. Lorenz's insights helped researchers better understand [turbulence](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/what-is-turbulent-flow), convection, and the [complex dynamics of fluid flow](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/what-is-hydrodynamics). His findings provided valuable insights into a wide range of disciplines, including [engineering](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/cfd-in-practice/why-study-fluid-dynamics), biology, and the study of natural phenomena. One of the key areas of fluid dynamics that Lorenz delved into was turbulence. Through his meticulous experiments and mathematical analyses, he revealed the inherent chaotic nature of turbulent flows. This discovery challenged the prevailing belief that [fluid motion](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/types-of-fluids) could be accurately described using linear equations. Lorenz's work revolutionized the understanding of turbulence, paving the way for new approaches to modeling and predicting fluid behavior. In addition to turbulence, Lorenz made significant contributions to the study of convection. His investigations into the transfer of heat through fluid motion led to the development of convection models that are still widely used today. Lorenz's work shed light on the complex interplay between temperature gradients, [fluid density variations](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/what-is-hydrostatics), and the resulting flow patterns. This understanding has proven crucial in fields such as meteorology, geophysics, and climate science. Furthermore, Lorenz's research on fluid dynamics had far-reaching implications beyond the confines of the laboratory. His findings had practical applications in engineering, particularly in the design of efficient transportation systems. By elucidating the underlying principles governing fluid flow, Lorenz enabled engineers to optimize the performance of pipelines, channels, and other fluid-carrying structures. His work also influenced the development of [aerodynamics](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/what-is-aerodynamics), leading to advancements in aircraft design and fuel efficiency. Edward Lorenz's legacy continues to resonate in the scientific community, as his work laid the foundation for chaos theory and nonlinear dynamics. His groundbreaking theories and principles have influenced numerous scientific disciplines, challenging established paradigms and inspiring further exploration of complex, unpredictable systems. Lorenz's intellect, creativity, and relentless pursuit of knowledge cemented his position as a true pioneer in the world of mathematics and meteorology.

Edward Lorenz was a renowned American mathematician and meteorologist whose groundbreaking work revolutionized the field of chaos theory and nonlinear dynamics. Born on May 23, 1917, in West Hartford, Connecticut, Lorenz developed a deep passion for mathematics at an early age. His insatiable curiosity and exceptional talent in the discipline laid the foundation for his extraordinary contributions to the scientific community. Throughout his illustrious career, Lorenz made significant discoveries and breakthroughs, leaving an indelible mark on the worlds of meteorology, mathematics, and physics.

## Early Life and Studies

Edward Lorenz's journey into the world of academia began at Dartmouth College, where he received his undergraduate degree in mathematics in 1938. Dartmouth College, nestled in the picturesque town of Hanover, New Hampshire, provided Lorenz with a solid foundation in mathematical principles and critical thinking skills. Surrounded by the vibrant fall foliage and the intellectual atmosphere of the Ivy League institution, Lorenz's passion for unraveling the mysteries of the universe began to take root.

His thirst for knowledge and desire to delve deeper into the intricacies of the natural world led him to pursue a Ph.D. in mathematics from Harvard University. Harvard, with its rich history and distinguished faculty, offered Lorenz an unparalleled academic environment. During his time at Harvard, Lorenz had the privilege of working under the guidance of mathematical prodigy and future Nobel laureate, George D. Birkhoff. Birkhoff's brilliance and mentorship had a profound impact on Lorenz, shaping his approach to mathematical research and instilling in him a deep appreciation for the beauty and elegance of the subject.

It was at Harvard that Lorenz first started exploring the intricate relationships between mathematics and atmospheric phenomena, laying the groundwork for his lifelong fascination with weather patterns and chaotic systems. The sprawling Harvard campus, with its iconic red-brick buildings and lush green lawns, provided Lorenz with the perfect backdrop for his intellectual pursuits. In the hallowed halls of Harvard's mathematics department, he delved into the complexities of fluid dynamics and began to unravel the [mathematical underpinnings of weather prediction](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/navier-stokes-equations).

After completing his doctoral studies in 1948, Lorenz embarked on a teaching career, joining the renowned faculty at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). MIT, located in the vibrant city of Cambridge, Massachusetts, was a hub of scientific innovation and intellectual curiosity. As a member of the MIT community, Lorenz found himself surrounded by brilliant minds and cutting-edge research, providing him with the ideal environment to further his scientific explorations.

During his tenure at MIT, Lorenz engaged in extensive research, studying atmospheric dynamics and investigating weather prediction models. He spent countless hours in the MIT Weather Radar Laboratory, meticulously analyzing data and developing mathematical models to understand the complex behavior of the atmosphere. Lorenz's relentless dedication to his work and his unwavering curiosity led him to make groundbreaking discoveries that would forever change the field of meteorology.

It was here, at MIT, that Lorenz's pioneering ideas took shape—a journey that would ultimately redefine the scientific community's understanding of the natural world. His groundbreaking research on chaos theory and the butterfly effect would revolutionize the way scientists approached the study of complex systems, transcending the boundaries of meteorology and influencing diverse fields such as physics, biology, and economics.

## Biggest Discoveries and Accomplishments

One of Edward Lorenz's most significant contributions to science came in the early 1960s when he set out to develop a weather model to predict future weather patterns. Armed with his thorough understanding of mathematical models, Lorenz sought to simplify the complexities of atmospheric behavior. Surprisingly, he discovered that even the most minute changes in the initial conditions of a dynamic system could cause drastic differences in the long-term outcomes.

This groundbreaking observation, which later became known as the "butterfly effect," revolutionized the way scientists approached the study of weather prediction. Lorenz demonstrated that tiny variations in the input parameters of mathematical models could lead to vast uncertainties in long-range forecasting. His work challenged the prevailing notion that precise and deterministic predictions were achievable, highlighting the inherent chaos and unpredictability present in nature's most complex systems.

As Lorenz delved deeper into the intricacies of weather patterns, he realized that the butterfly effect was not limited to just atmospheric behavior. It extended to a wide range of natural phenomena, from the growth of forests to the formation of galaxies. This realization opened up new avenues of research for scientists across various disciplines.

Furthermore, Lorenz's work had profound implications for the field of chaos theory. Prior to his discoveries, chaos was often associated with disorder and randomness. However, Lorenz's findings demonstrated that chaos could arise from deterministic systems, where small changes in initial conditions could lead to unpredictable outcomes. This concept challenged the traditional notion of cause and effect, paving the way for a deeper understanding of complex systems.

Building upon his groundbreaking research, Lorenz developed a set of equations known as the Lorenz attractor. These equations provided a visual representation of chaotic behavior, resembling the wings of a butterfly. The Lorenz attractor became an iconic symbol in the field of chaos theory, capturing the essence of nonlinear dynamics and the sensitivity to initial conditions.

Over the years, the butterfly effect has found applications in various fields beyond weather prediction. It has been used to study the dynamics of ecosystems, the spread of diseases, and even the behavior of financial markets. The recognition of the butterfly effect as a fundamental principle has led to the development of sophisticated mathematical models and computational techniques to better understand and predict the behavior of complex systems.

Lorenz's contributions to science extended beyond his groundbreaking discoveries. He was a passionate educator and mentor, inspiring generations of scientists to explore the mysteries of chaos and complexity. His work continues to shape the way we perceive and study the world around us, reminding us of the inherent beauty and unpredictability of nature's intricate tapestry.

## Contributions and Legacy in Fluid Dynamics

Beyond his contributions to weather prediction, Lorenz's pioneering research extended into the field of fluid dynamics. He explored the [behavior of fluids in motion](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/conservation-of-momentum-in-fluid-dynamics), uncovering intricate patterns that transcended traditional linear models. Lorenz's insights helped researchers better understand [turbulence](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/what-is-turbulent-flow), convection, and the [complex dynamics of fluid flow](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/what-is-hydrodynamics). His findings provided valuable insights into a wide range of disciplines, including [engineering](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/cfd-in-practice/why-study-fluid-dynamics), biology, and the study of natural phenomena.

One of the key areas of fluid dynamics that Lorenz delved into was turbulence. Through his meticulous experiments and mathematical analyses, he revealed the inherent chaotic nature of turbulent flows. This discovery challenged the prevailing belief that [fluid motion](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/types-of-fluids) could be accurately described using linear equations. Lorenz's work revolutionized the understanding of turbulence, paving the way for new approaches to modeling and predicting fluid behavior.

In addition to turbulence, Lorenz made significant contributions to the study of convection. His investigations into the transfer of heat through fluid motion led to the development of convection models that are still widely used today. Lorenz's work shed light on the complex interplay between temperature gradients, [fluid density variations](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/what-is-hydrostatics), and the resulting flow patterns. This understanding has proven crucial in fields such as meteorology, geophysics, and climate science.

Furthermore, Lorenz's research on fluid dynamics had far-reaching implications beyond the confines of the laboratory. His findings had practical applications in engineering, particularly in the design of efficient transportation systems. By elucidating the underlying principles governing fluid flow, Lorenz enabled engineers to optimize the performance of pipelines, channels, and other fluid-carrying structures. His work also influenced the development of [aerodynamics](https://www.resolvedanalytics.com/fluid-dynamics/what-is-aerodynamics), leading to advancements in aircraft design and fuel efficiency.

Edward Lorenz's legacy continues to resonate in the scientific community, as his work laid the foundation for chaos theory and nonlinear dynamics. His groundbreaking theories and principles have influenced numerous scientific disciplines, challenging established paradigms and inspiring further exploration of complex, unpredictable systems. Lorenz's intellect, creativity, and relentless pursuit of knowledge cemented his position as a true pioneer in the world of mathematics and meteorology.

SUMMARY:
Edward Lorenz was an American meteorologist and discoverer of the underlying mechanism of deterministic chaos, one of the principles of complexity.

FULL CONTENT:
![Encyclopedia Britannica](https://cdn.britannica.com/mendel/eb-logo/MendelNewThistleLogo.png)
![Encyclopedia Britannica](https://cdn.britannica.com/mendel/eb-logo/MendelNewThistleLogo.png)
![weather model of Edward Lorenz](https://cdn.britannica.com/93/26993-004-EC498639/Edward-Lorenz-function-weather-model-graph-z.jpg)
![Understand meteorologist Edward Lorenz's chaos theory](https://cdn.britannica.com/39/186439-138-C4556F71/Edward-Lorenz-contribution-chaos-theory.jpg?w=400&h=225&c=crop)
![Michael Faraday (L) English physicist and chemist (electromagnetism) and John Frederic Daniell (R) British chemist and meteorologist who invented the Daniell cell.](https://cdn.britannica.com/88/144788-131-ADACA20E/Michael-Faraday-John-Frederic-Daniell.jpg?w=200&h=200&c=crop)
![Equations written on blackboard](https://cdn.britannica.com/86/94086-131-0BAE374D/Equations-blackboard.jpg?w=200&h=200&c=crop)

# Edward Lorenz

Our editors will review what you’ve submitted and determine whether to revise the article.

**Edward Lorenz** (born May 23, 1917, [West Hartford](https://www.britannica.com/place/West-Hartford-Connecticut), [Connecticut](https://www.britannica.com/place/Connecticut), U.S.—died April 16, 2008, [Cambridge](https://www.britannica.com/place/Cambridge-Massachusetts), Massachusetts) was an American meteorologist and discoverer of the [underlying](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/underlying) mechanism of deterministic [chaos](https://www.britannica.com/science/chaos-theory), one of the principles of [complexity](https://www.britannica.com/science/complexity-scientific-theory).

After receiving degrees from [Dartmouth College](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Dartmouth-College) and [Harvard University](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Harvard-University) in [mathematics](https://www.britannica.com/science/mathematics), Lorenz turned to weather forecasting in 1942 with the U.S. Army Air Corps. After [World War II](https://www.britannica.com/event/World-War-II) he became a researcher at the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Massachusetts-Institute-of-Technology), where he earned a [master’s degree](https://www.britannica.com/topic/master-degree) (1943) and doctorate in [meteorology](https://www.britannica.com/science/meteorology) (1948) and stayed on as a professor. He later served as the head of the meteorology department.

![weather model of Edward Lorenz](https://cdn.britannica.com/93/26993-050-EF8FBFF5/Edward-Lorenz-function-weather-model-graph-z.jpg?w=300)

In the early 1960s Lorenz discovered that the [weather](https://www.britannica.com/science/weather) exhibits a nonlinear phenomenon known as [sensitive dependence on initial conditions](https://www.britannica.com/topic/sensitivity-to-initial-conditions) (*see* [chaos theory](https://www.britannica.com/science/chaos-theory)). He constructed a weather model showing that almost any two nearby starting points, indicating the current weather, will quickly [diverge](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/diverge) trajectories and will quite frequently end up in different “lobes,” which correspond to calm or stormy weather. He explained this phenomenon, which makes long-range [weather forecasting](https://www.britannica.com/science/weather-forecasting) impossible, to the public as the “[butterfly effect](https://www.britannica.com/science/butterfly-effect)”: in China a butterfly flaps its wings, leading to unpredictable changes in U.S. weather a few days later. For his groundbreaking work (his findings were published in 1963 in a paper entitled “Deterministic Nonperiodic Flow”), Lorenz shared the 1983 Crafoord Prize of the [Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Royal-Swedish-Academy-of-Sciences) and was awarded the 1991 Kyoto Prize.

![Equations written on blackboard](https://cdn.britannica.com/86/94086-131-0BAE374D/Equations-blackboard.jpg)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/dc/Edward_lorenz.jpg/250px-Edward_lorenz.jpg)

**Edward Norton Lorenz** (May 23, 1917 – April 16, 2008) was an American mathematician and [meteorologist](/wiki/Meteorologist "Meteorologist") who established the theoretical basis of weather and climate [predictability](/wiki/Predictability "Predictability"), as well as the basis for computer-aided [atmospheric physics](/wiki/Atmospheric_physics "Atmospheric physics") and [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Physics_today-2) He founded modern [chaos theory](/wiki/Chaos_theory "Chaos theory"), a branch of [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") focusing on the behavior of [dynamical systems](/wiki/Dynamical_system "Dynamical system") that are highly sensitive to [initial conditions](/wiki/Initial_conditions "Initial conditions").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

His discovery of [deterministic chaos](/wiki/Deterministic_chaos "Deterministic chaos") "profoundly influenced a wide range of basic sciences and brought about one of the most dramatic changes in mankind's view of nature since Sir Isaac Newton," according to the committee that awarded him the 1991 [Kyoto Prize](/wiki/Kyoto_Prize "Kyoto Prize") for basic sciences in the field of earth and planetary sciences.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Early life

Lorenz was born in 1917 in [West Hartford, Connecticut](/wiki/West_Hartford,_Connecticut "West Hartford, Connecticut").[[5]](#cite_note-mit_tt-5) He acquired an early love of science from both sides of his family. His father, Edward Henry Lorenz (1882-1956), majored in mechanical engineering at the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"), and his maternal grandfather, [Lewis M. Norton](/wiki/Lewis_M._Norton "Lewis M. Norton"), developed the first course in chemical engineering at [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT") in 1888. Meanwhile, his mother, Grace Peloubet Norton (1887-1943), instilled in Lorenz a deep interest in games, particularly chess.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Education

Lorenz received a bachelor's degree in mathematics from [Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College") in 1938 and a master's degree in mathematics from [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard "Harvard") in 1940. He worked as a [weather forecaster](/wiki/Weather_forecasting "Weather forecasting") for the [United States Army Air Forces](/wiki/United_States_Army_Air_Forces "United States Army Air Forces") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), leading him to pursue graduate studies in meteorology at the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology").[[7]](#cite_note-Kenneth_Chang-7) He earned both a master's and doctoral degree in [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology") from [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT") in 1943 and 1948.

His doctoral dissertation, titled "A Method of Applying the Hydrodynamic and Thermodynamic Equations to Atmospheric Models" and performed under advisor [James Murdoch Austin](/wiki/James_Murdoch_Austin "James Murdoch Austin"), described an application of fluid dynamical equations to the practical problem of predicting the motion of storms.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Scientific career

Lorenz spent the entirety of his scientific career at the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"). In 1948, he joined the MIT Department of Meteorology as a research scientist. In 1955, he became an assistant professor in the department and was promoted to professor in 1962. From 1977 to 1981, Lorenz served as head of the Department of Meteorology at MIT. In 1983, the MIT Department of Meteorology and Physical Oceanography merged with the Department of Geology to become the current MIT Department of Earth, Atmospheric and Planetary Sciences, where Lorenz remained a professor before becoming an emeritus professor in 1987.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Atmospheric circulation

In the late 1940s and early 1950s, Lorenz worked with [Victor Starr](/wiki/Victor_Starr "Victor Starr") on the General Circulation Project at MIT to understand the role the weather system played in determining the energetics of the general circulation of the atmosphere. From this work, in 1967, Lorenz published a landmark paper, titled "The Nature and Theory of the General Circulation of the Atmosphere," on [atmospheric circulation](/wiki/Atmospheric_circulation "Atmospheric circulation") from an energetic perspective, which advanced the concept of [available potential energy](/wiki/Available_potential_energy "Available potential energy").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Numerical weather prediction

In the 1950s, Lorenz became interested in and started work on [numerical weather prediction](/wiki/Numerical_weather_prediction "Numerical weather prediction"), which relied on computers to forecast weather by processing observational data on such things as temperature, pressure, and wind. This interest was sparked, in part, after a visit to the [Institute for Advanced Study](/wiki/Institute_for_Advanced_Study "Institute for Advanced Study") in Princeton, New Jersey, where he met [Jule Charney](/wiki/Jule_Charney "Jule Charney"), then head of the IAS's Meteorological Research Group and a leading dynamical meteorologist at the time.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11) (Charney would later join Lorenz at [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT") in 1957 as a professor of meteorology.)[[12]](#cite_note-MIT_News_article-12) In 1953, Lorenz took over leadership of a project at MIT that ran complex simulations of weather models that he used to evaluate statistical forecasting techniques.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11) By the late 1950s, Lorenz was skeptical of the appropriateness of the [linear](/wiki/Linear_system "Linear system") statistical models in meteorology, as most [atmospheric phenomena](/wiki/Atmospheric_phenomena "Atmospheric phenomena") involved in weather forecasting are [non-linear](/wiki/Non-linear "Non-linear").[[2]](#cite_note-Physics_today-2) It was during this time that his discovery of [deterministic chaos](/wiki/Deterministic_chaos "Deterministic chaos") came about.[[13]](#cite_note-Lorenz1961-13)

### Chaos theory

In 1961, Lorenz, with the assistance of [Margaret Hamilton](/wiki/Margaret_Hamilton_(software_engineer) "Margaret Hamilton (software engineer)")[[14]](#cite_note-14), was using a simple digital computer, a [Royal McBee](/wiki/Royal_McBee "Royal McBee") [LGP-30](/wiki/LGP-30 "LGP-30"), to simulate weather patterns by modeling 12 variables, representing things like temperature and wind speed. He wanted to see a sequence of data again, and to save time he started the simulation in the middle of its course. He did this by entering a printout of the data that corresponded to conditions in the middle of the original simulation. To his surprise, the weather that the machine began to predict was completely different from the previous calculation. The culprit: a rounded decimal number on the computer printout. The computer worked with 6-digit precision, but the printout rounded variables off to a 3-digit number, so a value like 0.506127 printed as 0.506. This difference is tiny, and the consensus at the time would have been that it should have no practical effect. However, Lorenz discovered that small changes in initial conditions produced large changes in long-term results.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Lorenz's discovery, which gave its name to [Lorenz attractors](/wiki/Lorenz_attractor "Lorenz attractor"), showed that even detailed atmospheric modelling cannot, in general, make precise long-term weather predictions. His work on the topic, assisted by [Ellen Fetter](/wiki/Ellen_Fetter "Ellen Fetter"), culminated in the publication of his 1963 paper "Deterministic Nonperiodic Flow" in *[Journal of the Atmospheric Sciences](/wiki/Journal_of_the_Atmospheric_Sciences "Journal of the Atmospheric Sciences")*, and with it, the foundation of [chaos theory](/wiki/Chaos_theory "Chaos theory").[[2]](#cite_note-Physics_today-2)[[13]](#cite_note-Lorenz1961-13) He states in that paper:

"Two states differing by imperceptible amounts may eventually evolve into two considerably different states ... If, then, there is any error whatever in observing the present state—and in any real system such errors seem inevitable—an acceptable prediction of an instantaneous state in the distant future may well be impossible....In view of the inevitable inaccuracy and incompleteness of weather observations, precise very-long-range forecasting would seem to be nonexistent."

His description of the [butterfly effect](/wiki/Butterfly_effect "Butterfly effect"), the idea that small changes can have large consequences, followed in 1969.[[2]](#cite_note-Physics_today-2)[[16]](#cite_note-Butterfly-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Quanta_Magazine_2019-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In the book "The Essence of Chaos," in the chapter "Our Chaotic Weather" from 1993, authored by Edward Lorenz and [Krzysztof Haman](/wiki/Krzysztof_Edward_Haman "Krzysztof Edward Haman"), the authors delved into the challenges of weather forecasting. The work discusses the consequences of chaos in the atmosphere and its impact on weather prediction. They describe a scenario in which meteorologists, in the computer age, generate [multiple long-term weather forecasts](/wiki/Ensemble_forecasting "Ensemble forecasting") based on different yet similar initial atmospheric conditions. Differences in the forecast results arise due to the sensitivity of the system to initial conditions.
[[22]](#cite_note-Lorenz1996-22)

Lorenz's insights on deterministic chaos resonated widely starting in the 1970s and 80s, when it spurred new fields of study in virtually every branch of science, from biology to geology to physics. In meteorology, it led to the conclusion that it may be fundamentally impossible to predict weather beyond two or three weeks with a reasonable degree of accuracy. However, the recognition of chaos has led to improvements in [weather forecasting](/wiki/Weather_forecasting "Weather forecasting"), as now forecasters recognize that measurements are imperfect and thus run many simulations starting from slightly different conditions, called [ensemble forecasting](/wiki/Ensemble_forecasting "Ensemble forecasting").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Of the seminal significance of Lorenz's work, [Kerry Emanuel](/wiki/Kerry_Emanuel "Kerry Emanuel"), a prominent meteorologist and climate scientist at MIT, has stated:[[24]](#cite_note-mit-news-24)

"By showing that certain deterministic systems have formal predictability limits, Ed put the last nail in the coffin of the Cartesian universe and fomented what some have called the third scientific revolution of the 20th century, following on the heels of relativity and quantum physics."

Late in his career, Lorenz began to be recognized with international accolades for the importance of his work on deterministic chaos. In 1983, along with colleague [Henry Stommel](/wiki/Henry_Stommel "Henry Stommel"), he was awarded the [Crafoord Prize](/wiki/Crafoord_Prize "Crafoord Prize") from the [Swedish Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Swedish_Academy_of_Sciences "Swedish Academy of Sciences"), considered to be nearly equal to a [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize").[[24]](#cite_note-mit-news-24) He was also awarded the [Kyoto Prize](/wiki/Kyoto_Prize "Kyoto Prize") for basic sciences in the field of earth and planetary sciences in 1991,[[25]](#cite_note-25) the [Buys Ballot Award](/wiki/Buys_Ballot_Medal "Buys Ballot Medal") in 2004, and the [Tomassoni Award](/wiki/Tomassoni_awards "Tomassoni awards") in 2008.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In 2018, a short documentary was made about Lorenz's immense scientific legacy on everything from how we predict weather to our understanding of the universe.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

A reprint book containing invited papers on butterfly effects was published to celebrate the 50th anniversary of the metaphorical butterfly effect.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Personal life and death

Later in life, Lorenz lived in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts") with his wife, Jane Loban (1919–2001), and their three children, Nancy, Cheryl, and Edward.[[7]](#cite_note-Kenneth_Chang-7)

He was an avid outdoorsman, who enjoyed hiking, climbing, and cross-country skiing. He kept up with these pursuits until very late in his life. On April 16, 2008, Lorenz died at his home in Cambridge from cancer at the age of 90.[[24]](#cite_note-mit-news-24)

## Legacy

Lorenz is remembered by colleagues and friends for his quiet demeanor, gentle humility, and love of nature.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11) He was described as "a genius with a soul of an artist" by his close friend and collaborator [Jule Charney](/wiki/Jule_Charney "Jule Charney").[[12]](#cite_note-MIT_News_article-12)

### The Lorenz Center

In 2011, The Lorenz Center, a climate think tank devoted to fundamental scientific inquiry, was founded at [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT") in honor of Lorenz and his pioneering work on [chaos theory](/wiki/Chaos_theory "Chaos theory") and [climate science](/wiki/Climate_science "Climate science").[[29]](#cite_note-The_Lorenz_Center-29)

### Centenary celebration

In February 2018, The Edward Lorenz Center and Henry Houghton Fund hosted a symposium, named MIT on Chaos and Climate, in honor of the 100th anniversary of the birth of Lorenz and [Charney](/wiki/Jule_Charney "Jule Charney").[[12]](#cite_note-MIT_News_article-12) The two-day event featured presentations from world-renowned experts on the many scientific contributions that the two pioneers made on the fields of [numerical weather prediction](/wiki/Numerical_weather_prediction "Numerical weather prediction"), [physical oceanography](/wiki/Physical_oceanography "Physical oceanography"), [atmospheric dynamics](/wiki/Atmospheric_dynamics "Atmospheric dynamics"), and [experimental fluid dynamics](/wiki/Fluid_dynamics "Fluid dynamics"), as well as the personal legacy they left behind of integrity, optimism, and collaboration. A video produced for the event highlights the indelible mark made by Charney and Lorenz on MIT and the field of meteorology as a whole.[[30]](#cite_note-Celebrating_the_Science_of_Jule_Charney_and_Ed_Lorenz-30)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Edward Lorenz was an American mathematician and meteorologist, known as the founder of chaos theory, who significantly advanced our understanding of weather prediction and complex systems.
Lorenz's pioneering studies in the early 1960s revealed that small changes in initial conditions could lead to vastly different outcomes, encapsulated in the concept of the butterfly effect.

Chaos theory evolved from Lorenz's work in meteorology, demonstrating that deterministic systems can exhibit unpredictable behavior, influencing diverse fields like biology, economics, and engineering.
